comments.text:
Fun video!  I discovered the nucleation effect myself at age four, by accident, (in 1955), by dropping pretzel salt into ginger ale at my grandparents' house.  I've played with this ever since, but I never thought of using popcorn!  Very cool!

Also, you can do the egg-into-the-bottle trick by putting some boiling water into the bottle, shaking it around to heat the air inside, then quickly dumping the water and sticking the egg on top... it takes awhile, though, for the science to happen, but you don't get ashes all over the egg!    It's a variation on Mr. Wizard's "The Invisible Giant" experiment.   

BTW - Repeating the process makes it possible to put multiple eggs in the bottle!  You do great work, Mark.
comments.author: @c.f.beeble

comments.text:
Chemistry teacher here. Your graphics team needs a science editor. There’s no such thing as a “surficant,” the word you seek is “surfactant,” and their chemistry is critical to living systems and most kinds of cleaning/washing.

P.s. To get food coloring off of skin try either or a foamy surfactant or a mildly abrasive paste such as either toothpaste or a mixture of baking soda, water and bit of dish soap, and alternating between these with longish distilled water soaks / rinse periods. Assume the color will transfer everywhere, so this demonstrates the value of PPE. Light solvents may also help such as ethanol, isopropanol or acetone. Figuring out the best process ALONE—one variable at a time—makes for more really good science!!!
comments.author: @STEAMerBear
